general_concerns: Believes that if she doesn’t get exactly 8 hours of sleep, her health will rapidly decline the next day.

Thinks taking a sleeping pill every night is the best long-term solution and that older barbiturate-type sleep medicines (e.g., Aprobarbital) are 'stronger and therefore safer' because they 'work better.'
Assumes alcohol (a glass of wine) is a harmless way to 'boost' the effect of a sleep medication.
Believes staying in bed longer and trying harder to sleep will fix insomnia, even if she’s awake for long periods.
Thinks snoring or sleep apnea only happen in older men, so it can’t be relevant to her sleep problems.
economic_concerns: Worries that non-medication approaches (therapy, sleep programs) will be expensive and take too long compared with a prescription.
Assumes generic medications are lower quality and less effective than brand-name sleep aids.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old HR coordinator. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with my days.” Early on, volunteer: long time to fall asleep, waking in the night and can’t return to sleep, unrefreshed mornings, daytime fatigue/irritability/brain fog.

If asked, share: ~2 months duration, often only 4–5 hours, racing thoughts about work/to-dos, phone use in bed, afternoon coffee, occasional afternoon naps, no chest pain or nighttime breathing trouble.

Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked about substances/meds/lifestyle: 2–3 evening drinks to “wind down” followed by waking, OTC sleep aid most nights with diminishing effect, little exercise/sunlight, later weekend sleep schedule.

Be somewhat resistant if asked about cannabis or mood: admit sometimes cannabis gummies; you’ve felt more down/anxious than you want to admit and worry something is wrong.

Express misconceptions: worry about thyroid because heart pounds when you can’t sleep; suspect sleep apnea because you wake up. Raise cost/time concerns about therapy/sleep programs; assume generics are less effective than brand-name sleep meds. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and open up a bit more.

medications: Aprobarbital 200 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg PO daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: SSRI-associated insomnia (sertraline)
Possible subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH with normal free T4) contributing to sleep disturbance
Perimenopausal transition with intermittent night sweats/hot flashes
High caffeine intake (3-4 cups coffee/day, last cup mid-afternoon)
Possible restless legs symptoms related to low ferritin
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (BMI 29.4, reports loud snoring per partner)

required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early-morning awakening
Daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, impaired concentration, mood disturbance)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week
Duration at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or <3 months for short-term insomnia if specified)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Not better explained by another sleep disorder, substance/medication effect, or uncontrolled medical/psychiatric condition

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (no evidence of hyperthyroidism contributing to insomnia)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range (no uncontrolled diabetes causing nocturia or sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Within reference range (no anemia contributing to fatigue/sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Within reference range (no iron deficiency suggesting restless legs contribution)

medications: Aprobarbital 200 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep for up to 7-10 days
non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as first-line
Sleep hygiene: consistent sleep/wake schedule, avoid naps, limit caffeine after noon, avoid alcohol close to bedtime, avoid heavy meals late, reduce evening screen time
Stimulus control: bed only for sleep/sex, leave bed if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes, return when sleepy
Sleep restriction therapy as part of CBT-I
Relaxation techniques (progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing)
Review and reduce contributing substances/medications (caffeine, nicotine, alcohol, decongestants, stimulants)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for response and adverse effects; earlier if worsening sedation, confusion, falls, or breathing problems

must_include: Insomnia is difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early with daytime impairment
Confirm frequency/duration criteria and adequate opportunity for sleep
Screen for common contributors (stress, caffeine/alcohol, pain, depression/anxiety, sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs symptoms)
CBT-I is first-line and has the best long-term benefit
If using aprobarbital: short-term use only, lowest effective dose, avoid nightly long-term use
Discuss risks: next-day sedation, impaired driving, falls, confusion, tolerance/dependence, withdrawal if abruptly stopped after prolonged use
Avoid alcohol and other sedatives while taking aprobarbital
Elicit patient goals and concerns about sleep medications (dependence, grogginess)
Review allergy history: sulfonamide antibiotics (TMP-SMX) urticaria and codeine severe nausea/vomiting; clarify no known barbiturate allergy
Provide clear instructions on when to seek help (severe sedation, breathing difficulty, confusion, falls)
Caffeine timing and reduction strategies
Consistent wake time and limiting time in bed to consolidate sleep
Bedroom environment optimization (dark, quiet, cool)
Avoiding alcohol as a sleep aid (can worsen sleep maintenance)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia symptoms, frequency/duration, daytime impairment, sleep opportunity, and screen for common contributors/comorbid sleep disorders before diagnosing.
Diagnose insomnia only after confirming criteria (sleep difficulty + adequate opportunity + daytime impairment) and considering contributors.
Provide first-line treatment counseling (CBT-I) and lifestyle/sleep hygiene education tailored to identified contributors.
Critical safety check prior to any prescribing; confirm allergy history including no known barbiturate allergy.
Critical safety check prior to prescribing; assess interactions with alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, other sedatives/CNS depressants.

Critical safety check prior to prescribing; ensure no contraindications (e.g., significant respiratory disease/sleep apnea risk, pregnancy, substance use disorder risk, high fall risk, severe hepatic impairment).
If medication is chosen, prescribe aprobarbital only short-term at the lowest effective dose with explicit safety counseling and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.
Arrange reassessment of response, adverse effects, and need for ongoing CBT-I; plan taper/stop if used beyond very short term.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 17-year-old male, anxious and tangential. Start with: “I keep hearing a voice talking about me, and it’s freaking me out.” Early on, volunteer: hearing a voice when alone, feeling watched/laughed at at school, poor sleep because your mind won’t shut off, feeling jumpy/on edge, missing grocery shifts. Speak in scattered, looping thoughts, jumping to worries about school/work.

If asked, add: the voice warns you you’re in danger/being set up; occasional shadowy flashes; trouble focusing and falling behind; isolating from friends; flat mood; skipping hygiene; moments of jumbled/racing thoughts.

Do NOT reveal unless directly asked with reassurance: command-like voice (“don’t trust them/leave now,” avoid specifics), thoughts of hurting yourself to make it stop (minimize; say you don’t want anyone to freak out), weed use (admit “a few times,” sometimes worse), fear of being seen as “crazy.” Keep hidden cognitive issues, sensory sensitivity, grades dropping before voices, and depersonalization unless the clinician probes.

Express misconceptions: maybe anxiety/ADHD/autism/depression/seizures; worry care/meds are unaffordable, public services unsafe, meds only on bad days; fear diagnosis harms jobs/insurance. If clinician is empathetic, you soften, talk more, and ask if you’re “going crazy.”

medications: Risperidone 2 mg PO nightly
Melatonin 3 mg PO nightly PRN insomnia
Polyethylene glycol 3350 17 g PO daily PRN constipation
comorbidities: Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (CREBBP mutation) with mild-to-moderate intellectual disability
confounders: Cannabis use (positive THC) contributing to psychosis-like symptoms and relapse risk
Intellectual disability and communication limitations from Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome complicating symptom characterization and baseline functioning
Sleep dysregulation/insomnia worsening paranoia and perceptual disturbances
Antipsychotic adverse effects (hyperprolactinemia, weight gain, dyslipidemia, borderline QTc prolongation) complicating medication selection and monitoring
Bullying/social stressors at school increasing anxiety, withdrawal, and negative symptoms

acceptable_alternatives: Substance/medication-induced psychotic disorder (must be ruled out)
Major depressive disorder with psychotic features (must be ruled out)
Bipolar disorder with psychotic features (must be ruled out)
required_evidence: Presence of psychotic symptoms (e.g., delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior, negative symptoms) with functional decline
Duration of illness consistent with schizophrenia spectrum (≥6 months for schizophrenia; if <6 months, consider schizophreniform as alternative)
Not better explained by mood disorder with psychotic features (psychosis not exclusively during mood episodes)
No evidence that symptoms are due to substances/medications or a medical condition (screening history/exam; consider toxicology and additional workup as indicated)
Age of onset in adolescence/early adulthood with early functional deterioration supports early-onset course

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal at baseline (no expected leukocytosis/leukopenia unless comorbid condition or medication effect)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Typically normal at baseline; monitor for hyponatremia if psychogenic polydipsia suspected
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Typically normal at baseline; assess hydration/renal function prior to medication choices

medications: Risperidone 0.5 mg PO nightly, increase by 0.5 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to 2-4 mg/day target
OR Aripiprazole 2 mg PO daily, increase to 10-15 mg/day as tolerated/needed
If acute agitation and severe distress: Lorazepam 0.5-1 mg PO/IM every 6 hours PRN (short-term, reassess daily)
non_pharmacological: Assess safety urgently: evaluate suicidality, homicidality, command hallucinations, ability to care for self; consider inpatient admission if high risk or unable to maintain safety
Family psychoeducation and involvement (with patient consent when possible); discuss relapse prevention and early warning signs
Coordinated specialty care/early psychosis program referral (psychotherapy, supported education/employment, case management)
CBT for psychosis and skills-based interventions as available
Substance use assessment and counseling (cannabis and stimulants strongly associated with relapse/worsening)
Sleep hygiene and routine stabilization; reduce stressors; avoid alcohol/illicit substances
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1 week after initiation or dose change (earlier within 24-72 hours if safety concerns), then every 1-2 weeks during titration

must_include: Working diagnosis on the schizophrenia spectrum and what that means (psychosis symptoms + functional impact)
Uncertainty and need to rule out other causes (substances, mood disorders, medical causes) and why
Expected course and importance of early treatment to improve outcomes
Rationale for antipsychotic medication as first-line and shared decision-making on choice
Start low/go slow titration, expected time to improvement (days to weeks for agitation; weeks for delusions/hallucinations)
Common side effects and what to do (sedation, dizziness, EPS/akathisia; weight/metabolic risk; prolactin-related effects with risperidone)
When to seek urgent care (worsening suicidality, severe agitation, rigidity/fever, severe allergic reaction)
Validate patient/family concerns about stigma, medication fears, and autonomy
Discuss safety planning and thresholds for hospitalization
Encourage adherence strategies and what to do if doses are missed
Confidentiality limits and involvement of family/supports
Avoid cannabis/stimulants and limit alcohol due to relapse risk and medication interactions
Sleep regularity and stress reduction strategies
Importance of follow-up appointments and early warning signs of relapse
Support resources (early psychosis program, therapy, school/work accommodations)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms of psychosis (hallucinations, delusions, disorganization), onset/duration, functional decline, and immediate safety risks (suicidality, homicidality, inability to care for self).
Clarify differential: mood symptoms (mania/depression), trauma, anxiety/OCD, developmental history, and medical/neurologic symptoms (seizure, head trauma, thyroid, autoimmune/infectious red flags).
Substance/medication history to rule out substance/medication-induced psychosis (cannabis, stimulants, hallucinogens, steroids), plus alcohol use.
Past psychiatric history, prior treatment response, family history of psychosis/bipolar, and psychosocial context (school/work functioning, supports, stressors).

Order baseline and rule-out labs appropriate for first-episode psychosis and antipsychotic initiation (e.g., CBC, CMP/electrolytes, fasting glucose or A1c, lipids, TSH; pregnancy test if applicable; urine toxicology; consider B12/folate, HIV/syphilis based on risk; baseline weight/BMI, BP, waist circumference).
Review lab/tox results to support/adjust diagnosis (substance-induced vs primary psychotic disorder) and ensure safe prescribing (metabolic, hepatic/renal issues).
Document working diagnosis on the schizophrenia spectrum (early-onset) with stated uncertainty and active rule-outs (substance-induced, mood disorder with psychotic features, medical causes).
Explain diagnosis milestone: what schizophrenia-spectrum means (psychosis + functional impact), uncertainty and why to rule out other causes, expected course and benefits of early treatment.
Critical safety check before prescribing any antipsychotic or adjunct medication.
Critical safety check before prescribing to identify interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging agents, CYP inhibitors/inducers, sedatives, stimulants, substances).

Medication-specific contraindication screen inputs (history of NMS, severe EPS, prolonged QT/arrhythmia, seizure disorder, diabetes/metabolic syndrome risk, hyperprolactinemia concerns, pregnancy/lactation, orthostasis).
Critical contraindication check before prescribing chosen medication.
Initiate first-line antipsychotic with shared decision-making; start low/go slow; provide clear titration and monitoring plan.
Explain treatment milestone: rationale for antipsychotic, expected time to improvement, common side effects and management, and urgent warning signs requiring immediate care.

Address concerns milestone: validate stigma/med fears/autonomy; safety planning and hospitalization thresholds; adherence strategies and missed-dose guidance; confidentiality limits and family involvement.

Lifestyle/relapse prevention milestone: avoid cannabis/stimulants, limit alcohol; sleep/stress strategies; early warning signs; resources (early psychosis program, therapy, school/work accommodations).
Arrange close follow-up (e.g., within 1 week initially) for symptom response, side effects (EPS/akathisia), vitals/weight, metabolic monitoring, and safety reassessment.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting plan, and ensuring emergency instructions and follow-up are in place.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 62-year-old woman who is cooperative and speaks clearly. Start by saying: “I keep getting sudden electric-shock pains on the right side of my face.” Volunteer early: brief, stabbing shocks in the right cheek/upper jaw lasting seconds, coming in clusters; brushing teeth or touching your face can trigger it; you’re scared to eat or talk during flares. If asked, add: between shocks you feel normal; triggers include chewing, talking, smiling, washing face, makeup, light breeze; cold air/drinks worsen it; always one-sided; no fever, no facial weakness, no rash/blisters. Hold back unless specifically asked: you’ve avoided brushing/chewing that side, eating less, sleeping worse, feeling anxious/on edge. Only if asked about meds/pain control, admit you’ve been taking extra pain pills more often than you should “to prevent an attack,” and you worry they’ll think you’re drug-seeking. Express misconceptions: you thought it was a tooth or sinus issue; mild headache after makes you worry about migraines. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge relief and say you’re scared and want help but need low-cost, “basic” care; you’re worried about paying for labs/imaging and want to skip blood tests if possible.

Start with: “I get out of breath really easily now, even just walking across the room.” Early in the visit, volunteer: constant fatigue/low stamina, ankle swelling by day’s end, dry cough for months, and that your kidney function is low. Exclusion/low likelihood of alternative primary parenchymal lung disease causing PH (e.g., COP/AIP) and connective tissue disease-associated ILD/PAH (e.g., RA) based on history, exam, and targeted testing

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal or mildly elevated (secondary erythrocytosis possible in chronic hypoxemia); anemia may also be present depending on comorbidities
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Often normal; may be reduced if on anticoagulation-related complications or comorbid disease; monitor for thrombocytopenia before/after anticoagulation
expected_lab_results.WBC: Typically normal (helps argue against acute infection/inflammatory flare as primary driver)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated or high-normal consistent with renal insufficiency and/or reduced renal perfusion
expected_lab_results.eGFR (CKD-EPI): Reduced (<60 mL/min/1.73m2 suggests CKD; severity guides anticoagulant selection/dosing)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be low-normal or mildly low if right heart failure/volume overload; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Potassium: May be normal; can be elevated in CKD and/or with RAAS inhibitors/diuretics if used

medications: Apixaban 10 mg PO twice daily for 7 days, then 5 mg PO twice daily (long-term anticoagulation; adjust/avoid per renal function, age, weight, and bleeding risk)

Riociguat 1 mg PO three times daily, titrate by 0.5 mg every 2 weeks as tolerated to max 2.5 mg PO three times daily (for inoperable CTEPH or persistent/recurrent PH after pulmonary endarterectomy; only after specialist evaluation; avoid if hypotension)
Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily (if volume overload/right heart failure; titrate to symptoms and renal function/electrolytes)
Oxygen therapy to maintain SpO2 >= 92% (or per individualized target if chronic hypercapnia present)
non_pharmacological: Refer urgently to a CTEPH expert center for operability assessment and consideration of pulmonary endarterectomy (curative in appropriate candidates)
If not surgical candidate or residual PH: consider balloon pulmonary angioplasty at expert center
Structured, supervised exercise/rehabilitation program once stable
Vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal) and smoking cessation counseling if applicable
Evaluate and manage contributing factors: sleep-disordered breathing, chronic hypoxemia, and right heart failure
Assess bleeding risk and counsel on anticoagulation precautions given abnormal von Willebrand factor; coordinate with hematology if significant bleeding history
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to review anticoagulation tolerance, renal function/electrolytes, and volume status; specialist CTEPH center evaluation within 2-4 weeks; repeat functional/hemodynamic assessment per specialist (often 3-6 months after therapy changes)

must_include: CTEPH is high blood pressure in the lung arteries caused by chronic organized blood clots/scar tissue obstructing pulmonary vessels
This is different from acute pulmonary embolism and can persist or develop months after clots
Why key tests are needed: V/Q scan and right-heart catheterization; imaging to map clot burden
Discuss important alternative diagnoses (COP/AIP/RA-related lung disease) and how testing distinguishes them
Anticoagulation is lifelong in most CTEPH to prevent new clots; review bleeding warning signs given abnormal von Willebrand factor
Definitive therapies: pulmonary endarterectomy (potentially curative) and/or balloon pulmonary angioplasty at specialized centers
Medical therapy (e.g., riociguat) is for inoperable disease or persistent PH after surgery and requires specialist oversight
Renal insufficiency affects medication choice/dosing and requires lab monitoring
Contrast allergy: prior immediate pruritus/wheeze suggests IgE-like reaction; discuss alternative imaging (V/Q) and, if contrast imaging is essential, premedication and monitored setting
Sulfonamide antibiotic allergy: avoid TMP-SMX; clarify that non-antibiotic sulfonamides may or may not cross-react and should be considered cautiously case-by-case
When to seek urgent care: worsening shortness of breath, syncope, chest pain, hemoptysis, severe bleeding, signs of stroke
Activity pacing and supervised rehab; avoid sudden overexertion until stabilized
Avoid estrogen-containing therapies and address VTE risk factors (immobility, dehydration, smoking)
Adherence to anticoagulation and follow-up labs; inform all clinicians/dentists about anticoagulant use
Diet/OTC counseling: avoid NSAIDs if possible due to bleeding and renal risk; review supplements/herbals that increase bleeding risk

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key CTEPH history and current severity: chronic/progressive dyspnea (>3 months), exercise intolerance, syncope/presyncope, chest pain, hemoptysis; prior PE/DVT timeline and anticoagulation adherence; risk factors (surgery, immobility, cancer, thrombophilia, pregnancy/estrogen); functional class; oxygen use; bleeding history (given abnormal vWF); renal disease history; medication list; allergies including iodinated contrast and sulfonamide antibiotics.

Critical before ordering: verify and characterize contrast reaction (immediate pruritus/wheeze suggests IgE-like) and sulfonamide antibiotic allergy; document reaction type/severity and any prior contrast premedication tolerance.

Baseline safety and pre-therapy evaluation: CBC (anemia/platelets), CMP/creatinine/eGFR (renal insufficiency affects imaging contrast and medication dosing), LFTs, INR/PT/aPTT as appropriate for current anticoagulant, BNP/NT-proBNP for RV strain, pregnancy test if applicable; consider iron studies if anemia suspected.

Order initial diagnostic tests consistent with CTEPH workup: V/Q scan as preferred screening (especially with contrast allergy/renal insufficiency), transthoracic echocardiogram for PH/RV function, ECG, chest X-ray; plan definitive hemodynamics with right-heart catheterization and pulmonary angiography/CTPA mapping in specialized setting if needed.

Interpret results to support CTEPH: mismatched perfusion defects on V/Q; echo signs of PH/RV dilation/dysfunction; labs for renal function/bleeding risk; use results to justify referral and definitive testing.

State working diagnosis (suspected/confirmed CTEPH depending on available results) and explicitly address key differentials (e.g., COP/AIP/RA-related lung disease) and how the ordered tests distinguish them (V/Q vs parenchymal imaging; RHC hemodynamics; imaging to map chronic thromboembolic lesions).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (organized chronic clot/scar obstruction causing PH; differs from acute PE; tests needed: V/Q, RHC, mapping); explain treatment (lifelong anticoagulation in most; bleeding warnings with abnormal vWF; definitive therapies PEA/BPA at expert centers; riociguat for inoperable/persistent PH; renal insufficiency affects dosing/monitoring); address concerns (contrast allergy alternatives/premedication; sulfonamide allergy guidance; urgent symptoms); lifestyle counseling (activity pacing/rehab; avoid estrogen; VTE risk factor modification; anticoag adherence and lab follow-up; avoid NSAIDs and review supplements).

Critical before prescribing: assess interactions with current meds and intended anticoagulant/riociguat (e.g., strong CYP/P-gp modulators for DOACs; antiplatelets/NSAIDs increasing bleeding; nitrates/PDE5 inhibitors contraindicated with riociguat).

Initiate/continue appropriate anticoagulation unless contraindicated; choose agent mindful of renal function and bleeding risk; avoid contraindicated combinations (e.g., riociguat with PDE5 inhibitors/nitrates). Provide clear monitoring plan and bleeding precautions.

Arrange urgent referral to a specialized CTEPH center for operability assessment (PEA/BPA) and definitive hemodynamic testing; schedule short-interval follow-up to review results, renal function, anticoagulation tolerance, and symptom trajectory.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

profile.occupation: Warehouse forklift operator

patient.chief_complaint: My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping more and more.

volunteer: My legs feel tight and stiff, especially when I try to walk faster or on uneven ground.
I trip a lot because my toes catch the floor.
I feel like my walk has turned into a slow, scissoring shuffle.
Going down stairs is scary because my legs don’t move the way I want.
My ankles feel weak and my feet sometimes slap the ground.
if_asked: It’s been gradually getting worse over years, not days.
My legs get jumpy with sudden sounds or if someone bumps me.
My feet sometimes cramp at night and my calves feel tight in the mornings.
I’ve noticed my ankles don’t seem to ‘kick’ the way they used to when the doctor taps them.
I get tired quickly when walking, even though my breathing feels fine.
My arms feel stiff too, and my hands can feel clumsy when I’m tired.
My neck and back feel weak and I have trouble holding good posture for long.
hidden: I’ve started holding onto walls or furniture at home without thinking about it.
I avoid long walks or crowded places because I’m afraid I’ll fall, but I don’t usually say that out loud.
My shoes wear out oddly at the toes because they drag.
I’ve had more trouble getting up from low chairs because my legs won’t relax.
I sometimes feel like my legs are ‘fighting me’ when I try to turn or pivot.
resistant: I’ve had a few accidents where I couldn’t hold my pee in time, especially when I’m out of the house.
Sex has been harder because my legs spasm or feel too stiff, and it’s embarrassing to talk about.
I’ve been feeling down and frustrated because I’m losing independence, but I try to hide it.
misleading: I think it might be a pinched nerve in my back because I get low back soreness after walking.
I wondered if it’s multiple sclerosis because I read online that stiffness and tripping can be MS.
Sometimes my feet feel a bit tingly after sitting a long time, so I thought it could be neuropathy.
I tried magnesium and stretching videos because I thought it was just ‘bad cramps’ or dehydration.
noise: I’ve had seasonal allergies with sneezing and itchy eyes.
I get occasional tension headaches after long screen time.
My stomach gets bloated with certain foods like dairy.
I have a small patch of dry skin on my elbow that comes and goes.

progressive_reveal.symptom: It’s been gradually getting worse over years, not days.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about when it started and how fast it’s changing.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve noticed my ankles don’t seem to ‘kick’ the way they used to when the doctor taps them.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about past exam findings or checks reflex history; patient recalls being told the heel reflex was hard to get.

progressive_reveal.symptom: My arms feel stiff too, and my hands can feel clumsy when I’m tired.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks whether symptoms are only in the legs or anywhere else.

progressive_reveal.symptom: My neck and back feel weak and I have trouble holding good posture for long.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about trunk control, posture, or core weakness.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve had a few accidents where I couldn’t hold my pee in time, especially when I’m out of the house.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks directly about bladder or bowel changes in a nonjudgmental way.

progressive_reveal.symptom: Sex has been harder because my legs spasm or feel too stiff, and it’s embarrassing to talk about.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician normalizes the topic and asks about how symptoms affect intimacy.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I avoid long walks or crowded places because I’m afraid I’ll fall, but I don’t usually say that out loud.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about falls, near-falls, and activity avoidance.

general_concerns: Thinks hereditary spastic paraplegia is basically the same as ALS and assumes he will “end up on a ventilator soon.”
Believes the absent Achilles reflex means his spinal cord is “dead” and that walking is already irreversible no matter what he does.
Assumes symptoms are mainly from “pinched nerves” from lifting at work and that a chiropractor or back surgery would fix the problem.
Thinks genetic testing will automatically go into a public record and be used to deny jobs or insurance.
Believes stretching and exercise will worsen the disease and prefers to avoid physical therapy to “not wear out the nerves.”
economic_concerns: Worries that admitting weakness or needing a cane will get him fired or taken off shifts.
Avoids follow-up visits and testing because he expects large out-of-pocket bills and fears collections.
Believes disability benefits are the only realistic option and thinks the clinician should “just sign the paperwork” without further evaluation.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, 34, a warehouse forklift operator with a high-school education. You speak vaguely and act evasive, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start by saying your legs have been getting stiffer and you keep tripping more. Volunteer: legs feel tight/stiff (worse when walking fast/uneven ground), toes catch the floor, slow scissoring shuffle, stairs feel unsafe, ankles feel weak and feet sometimes slap.
If asked for details, admit it’s been slowly worsening for years; legs get “jumpy” with loud sounds or bumps; night cramps and morning calf tightness; you tire quickly; occasional arm stiffness/clumsy hands when tired; weak posture/neck/back. Don’t volunteer that you hold walls, avoid crowds/long walks, shoes wear at toes, trouble rising from low chairs, or difficulty turning—only confirm if specifically asked.
Resist discussing urinary accidents, sexual problems, and low mood; deny at first, then reluctantly admit if asked clearly and nonjudgmentally.
Express misconceptions: think it’s a pinched back nerve; worry about MS/neuropathy; tried magnesium/stretching.
If clinician shows empathy, soften briefly but pivot to fear of job loss and costs; push for disability paperwork and avoid tests unless reassured about necessity and expenses.

medications: Baclofen 10 mg PO three times daily
Gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime
Polyethylene glycol 3350 17 g PO daily as needed for constipation
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO daily
comorbidities: Chronic constipation related to immobility and antispasticity therapy
confounders: Borderline vitamin B12 status with elevated methylmalonic acid (can mimic/worsen myelopathy and gait disturbance)
Cervical spondylotic myelopathy risk (can present with spasticity and gait impairment; may coexist)
Medication effects (baclofen/gabapentin) contributing to weakness/sedation and functional decline
Deconditioning from progressive immobility affecting strength and reflex assessment (including absent Achilles reflex)

acceptable_alternatives: Subacute combined degeneration (vitamin B12 deficiency myelopathy)
required_evidence: Progressive spastic paraparesis with upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, extensor plantar responses) predominantly affecting lower extremities
Chronic course without sensory level or acute stepwise deficits
Family history of similar gait/spasticity symptoms OR confirmed pathogenic variant on genetic testing (if available)
Absence of prominent lower motor neuron signs (e.g., diffuse atrophy, fasciculations) to argue against classic ALS
Exclusion of structural spinal cord lesion (MRI spine) and inflammatory demyelination (MRI brain/spine ± CSF) as appropriate

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal baseline; required for dosing if renal impairment
expected_lab_results.CMP - AST: Typically normal baseline; useful prior to hepatically metabolized meds
expected_lab_results.CMP - ALT: Typically normal baseline; useful prior to hepatically metabolized meds

medications: Baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily; titrate by 5 mg/dose every 3-7 days as tolerated (typical 20-80 mg/day)
If inadequate or not tolerated: Tizanidine 2 mg PO at bedtime; titrate by 2-4 mg every 3-7 days (max 36 mg/day) with liver enzyme monitoring
If focal spasticity: OnabotulinumtoxinA IM to affected muscle groups per spasticity pattern and dosing guidelines every 12 weeks
non_pharmacological: Referral to neurology (movement disorders/neuromuscular) for confirmation, phenotyping (pure vs complex HSP), and genetic counseling/testing
Physical therapy focused on stretching, strengthening, gait training, balance, and spasticity management
Occupational therapy for mobility aids, home safety evaluation, and ADL optimization
Assistive devices as needed (AFOs for gait mechanics/foot drop if present, cane/walker), fall-risk mitigation
Regular exercise program (low-impact aerobic + flexibility) and spasticity trigger management (hydration, avoid prolonged immobility)
Screen and treat complications: bladder dysfunction, constipation, pain, depression/anxiety, sleep disturbance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptom control, side effects, and function in 4-6 weeks after initiating/titrating antispasticity therapy; earlier (1-2 weeks) if significant sedation, weakness, or falls occur

must_include: HSP is a group of inherited disorders causing progressive stiffness/spasticity and weakness mainly in the legs due to upper motor neuron pathway involvement
Diagnosis is clinical and often supported by family history and/or genetic testing, while ruling out treatable mimics (spinal cord compression, MS, B12 deficiency)
Course is typically slowly progressive; severity and associated features vary by subtype
No curative therapy; treatment focuses on symptom control (spasticity), mobility, and preventing complications
Medication goals and common adverse effects (baclofen/tizanidine: sedation, dizziness, weakness; tizanidine: hypotension and liver enzyme elevation)
Importance of gradual titration and not stopping baclofen abruptly due to withdrawal risk
Review fall risk and safety plan, including when to call urgently (sudden worsening, new sensory level, bowel/bladder retention, severe weakness)
Discuss genetic implications and offer genetic counseling for family planning and testing of relatives as appropriate
Set expectations for monitoring response and adjusting therapy based on function and side effects
Regular stretching and strengthening are core to maintaining mobility and reducing spasticity
Avoid triggers that worsen spasticity (infections, dehydration, prolonged immobility); maintain bowel/bladder routines
Home modifications and assistive devices can improve independence and reduce falls

Explain treatment approach (no cure; symptom management; PT/OT; spasticity meds), including titration and key adverse effects and warning about abrupt baclofen cessation.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication for spasticity or related symptoms.
Critical safety check before prescribing, especially for CNS depressants, antihypertensives, and hepatotoxic agents (e.g., tizanidine interactions).
Obtain current medication list (including OTC/supplements), alcohol/cannabis use, and comorbidities to support contraindication assessment if not already captured.
Initiate evidence-based symptomatic therapy (e.g., baclofen or tizanidine) with gradual titration plan and monitoring; avoid if contraindications or interactions are present.
Provide lifestyle/rehab education: stretching/strengthening, trigger avoidance, bowel/bladder routines, fall prevention, assistive devices, and when to seek urgent care.
Arrange follow-up to monitor function, spasticity, side effects (sedation, hypotension), and adjust therapy; consider referral for genetic counseling/testing and PT/OT.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

comorbidities: Alcohol use disorder (in remission on disulfiram)
New-onset hypertension (likely secondary to nephritic syndrome)
confounders: Recent streptococcal pharyngitis 2-3 weeks prior (supports post-infectious GN but may be underreported)

High-protein workout supplements/creatine use (may confound creatinine interpretation)
Trace leukocyte esterase/WBCs without nitrites (can mimic UTI but overall picture favors glomerulonephritis)

acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury due to nephritis (undifferentiated)
Acute glomerulonephritis (if supportive urinary findings are present)
Acute tubular injury (if nephritis evidence is lacking)
required_evidence: Acute or subacute rise in serum creatinine with reduced eGFR consistent with AKI
Clinical context suggestive of nephritis (e.g., systemic symptoms or recent medication exposure) and exclusion of obvious pre-renal/obstructive causes
Electrolyte/acid-base pattern compatible with renal impairment (e.g., hyperkalemia and/or low bicarbonate)
Need for urinalysis with microscopy (hematuria/proteinuria/casts) to support nephritis classification (even if not provided in current labs)

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Elevated from baseline (AKI range) with upward trend if ongoing injury
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated; BUN:creatinine ratio variable (not reliably pre-renal)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to elevated; may be significantly elevated in moderate/severe AKI
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal; may be low if volume overload or high free-water intake
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Normal to elevated (hyperchloremic component possible if non-anion-gap acidosis)
expected_lab_results.Calcium: Usually normal; may be low in advanced renal dysfunction

medications: Stop suspected offending nephrotoxin(s) immediately (no new nephrotoxic drugs)
Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate 10 g PO TID for 48 hours then 10 g PO daily (if hyperkalemia present and patient can take PO)
Furosemide 20-40 mg IV/PO once, then titrate (if volume overloaded and producing urine)
Sodium bicarbonate 650-1300 mg PO TID (if metabolic acidosis with low bicarbonate and patient can take PO)
non_pharmacological: Assess volume status; provide isotonic fluids if hypovolemic, or fluid restriction/diuresis if hypervolemic
Renal-protective strategy: avoid NSAIDs, iodinated contrast, ACEi/ARB initiation during active AKI unless compelling indication
Strict input/output monitoring and daily weights
Repeat renal function and electrolytes within 24 hours (sooner if potassium/acidosis severe)
Urgent nephrology consult if rapidly rising creatinine, severe electrolyte/acid-base derangements, suspected glomerulonephritis, or need for renal biopsy
Consider renal ultrasound to exclude obstruction (especially if unclear etiology)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck BMP (creatinine, BUN, electrolytes, bicarbonate) within 24 hours; close outpatient follow-up within 3-7 days after stabilization/discharge, sooner if worsening labs or symptoms

Uncertainty of exact subtype without urine studies; need for additional testing (urinalysis, urine protein/creatinine, possible serologies) and monitoring
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care (decreased urine output, shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, severe weakness/palpitations)
Immediate goals: protect kidneys, correct dangerous electrolytes/acid-base issues, and remove potential triggers
Plan for repeat labs and when escalation to hospital/nephrology is needed
If hyperkalemia suspected/confirmed: explain arrhythmia risk and urgency of treatment/monitoring
Review current medication list including Disulfiram; emphasize avoiding alcohol while on disulfiram and to report any new rash, fever, or reduced urine output
Discuss avoidance of over-the-counter NSAIDs and herbal supplements that can worsen kidney function

Hydration guidance tailored to volume status (do not overhydrate if swollen/short of breath)

Sick-day rules: hold potentially kidney-stressing meds only under clinician guidance and seek prompt evaluation if vomiting/diarrhea or poor intake

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit history focused on nephritis/AKI: onset, urine changes, edema, HTN symptoms, systemic symptoms (fever, rash, arthralgias), recent infections, new meds/toxins (NSAIDs/herbals), volume status, and red flags (oliguria, dyspnea, chest pain, confusion). Confirm disulfiram use and penicillin allergy details.

Review results to risk-stratify: degree of AKI, hyperkalemia/acidosis, hematuria/proteinuria/casts, infection markers; determine need for urgent ED/hospitalization vs outpatient monitoring and nephrology referral.

Communicate working diagnosis (suspected nephritis/glomerulonephritis vs interstitial nephritis) and acknowledge uncertainty pending urine studies/serologies; identify complications (e.g., hyperkalemia, fluid overload) if present.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm/document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and any other allergies to avoid inadvertent exposure.

Critical safety check before prescribing; evaluate interactions with current meds including disulfiram (avoid alcohol-containing products; check for interacting antibiotics/psych meds) and renal-dose considerations.

Initiate appropriate therapy based on results and severity: avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs), treat infection if indicated with allergy-appropriate and renal-adjusted antibiotic, manage hypertension/edema if safe, and escalate urgently if hyperkalemia/acidosis/uremia/volume overload. Do not prescribe contraindicated nephrotoxic agents.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and uncertainty, treatment goals and escalation plan (including hyperkalemia arrhythmia risk if suspected/confirmed), medication/OTC counseling (NSAIDs/herbals), disulfiram precautions, allergy documentation, hydration guidance, low-potassium diet if needed, and sick-day rules.

Arrange repeat labs and follow-up timing based on risk (often 24–72h for AKI/hyperkalemia risk) and nephrology referral if significant proteinuria/hematuria/casts, rising creatinine, or systemic features.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, red-flag return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 16-year-old student and part-time grocery cashier. Open with: hands and feet have hurt and swollen for months and your fingers look like they’re changing shape. Early in the visit, volunteer: daily aching with swelling around knuckles/wrists, ~1 hour morning stiffness, trouble gripping jars/steering wheel, some fingers look shorter/squished, and small firm lumps near elbows and backs of hands. If asked, add: worse with use/end of day, occasional warmth/tenderness in wrists/ankles during flares, midfoot pain and tight shoes, worsening range of motion, and occasional hoarse voice/tight throat on bad days. Do not mention unless specifically asked about other symptoms: getting more short of breath on stairs, dry gritty eyes, easy gum bleeding/loose teeth, and winter episodes of blue cold fingers that throb when warming. Only reveal intimacy avoidance if asked about mood/relationships. Express misconceptions: worry it’s rheumatoid arthritis; also wondered about gout or carpal tunnel. Bring up cost concerns when tests/referrals/therapy are discussed. If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, thank them, and share more details.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy (MONA syndrome)
required_evidence: Progressive osteolysis affecting carpal/tarsal bones with deforming arthropathy
Subcutaneous nodulosis (firm nodules) and/or periarticular soft-tissue nodules
Inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) normal or only mildly elevated relative to degree of joint destruction
Absence of cytopenias or alternative systemic inflammatory features on CBC/CMP that would better support infection/malignancy
Clinical course consistent with chronic, progressive, non-infectious osteolysis rather than acute septic arthritis
Consideration of genetic confirmation (e.g., MMP2-related disease) as part of diagnostic workup (not required in available labs)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal or mild anemia of chronic disease; no pattern suggesting hemolysis or malignancy
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal (no leukocytosis to suggest acute infection)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal (may be mildly elevated if chronic inflammation)

medications: Naproxen 250-500 mg PO twice daily with food as needed for pain/inflammation (age/weight-adjust if pediatric)
Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; weight-based if pediatric)
non_pharmacological: Referral to rheumatology and orthopedics for coordinated management of progressive osteolysis/arthropathy
Physical and occupational therapy for joint protection, range-of-motion, strengthening, splinting, and assistive devices
Activity modification to reduce high-impact loading; individualized exercise plan
Pain management plan emphasizing non-opioid modalities; consider multidisciplinary pain clinic if refractory
Genetic counseling and consideration of confirmatory genetic testing (e.g., MMP2) and family screening as appropriate
Imaging follow-up (targeted radiographs/MRI as clinically indicated) to monitor progression and complications
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, function, and medication tolerability in 4-6 weeks; earlier (1-2 weeks) if worsening pain, new swelling, fever, or functional decline

must_include: Condition involves progressive bone loss (osteolysis) and joint damage that can mimic inflammatory arthritis
It is typically chronic and not caused by infection; routine labs may be normal or only mildly elevated
Expectations: symptoms can progress; goals are pain control, preserving function, and monitoring complications
Need for specialist evaluation and possible genetic confirmation/family implications
Focus on symptom control (NSAIDs/acetaminophen) and function-preserving therapy (PT/OT, splints, assistive devices)
Medication use instructions and key adverse effects (NSAID GI bleeding/ulcer risk, kidney risk; acetaminophen liver toxicity with excess dosing)
When to avoid NSAIDs (history of ulcer/bleeding, significant kidney disease, anticoagulant use, uncontrolled hypertension) and to take with food
Plan for referrals (rheumatology/orthopedics) and monitoring over time
Acknowledge impact on daily activities and pain; invite questions about prognosis and inheritance
Red flags requiring urgent care: fever, rapidly worsening joint swelling/redness, inability to bear weight/use limb, new neurologic symptoms, severe uncontrolled pain
Shared decision-making about pain control options and therapy goals
Joint protection strategies (avoid high-impact activities; ergonomic adaptations)
Importance of consistent PT/OT and adherence to splinting/assistive devices to prevent deformity and maintain function
Medication safety: avoid duplicate acetaminophen-containing products; avoid alcohol excess with acetaminophen
Encourage follow-up and tracking of symptom changes and functional limitations
